description.text:
## Description

Okay, I think it's time to make this PR a tad more visible. **~~This PR is not yet ready for review!~~ It's ready now!** You may, however, if you're curious, build it and run it in test mode (`yarn test-gui --clean`) to have a first look.

I must admit, I got really pissed with tons of pieces of the codebase and decided to clean up even completely unrelated parts (such as the FSAL). That explains the hefty amount of changed files (188 at the time of writing).

Was this a clever decision?

No. Not at all.

Was it a satisfying action?

You bet!

## Changes

The following ist is guaranteed to be incomplete, but I'll give it a shot:

1. Exchanged Codemirror 5 with Codemirror 6 (currently: Only the main editor, still todo: the smaller code editors)
2. While migrating the plugins from 5->6 gave most of them a facelift, thanks to, first, a MUCH better API, and second, a godsent AST
3. **Documents are now managed CENTRALLY in the DocumentsProvider**. Why does that matter? Because now there is **one** source of truth for all documents. This makes the shutdown logic much simpler, keeps every editor leaf in sync, and a ton of other benefits. Plus, it's fancy!
4. I finally decided to merge the Meta-descriptors in the FSAL with the "regular" ones. The distinction into meta and non-meta descriptors didn't make sense and is 100% on me. Now it's much better.
5. Disentangled the FSAL, Documents, and Target providers which previously were much tighter entangled. This had the problem of a thousand and one hard-to-track side-effects between the providers.
6. The FSAL now specifically has two utility functions that will return a descriptor for a random path on disk (as long as it's supported) and one that will return a (supported) file's contents. No more poking around in the guts of a provider.

## Additional information

Still TODO:

- [x] Move the CodeEditor to Codemirror 6
- [x] Re-implement the TableEditor
- [x] Allow for granular control of the renderers (currently hardcoded due to limitations in my imagination regarding the new config system)
- [x] Put the extensions into compartments for easy-ish reconfiguration of existing documents
- [x] Re-enable spell-checking
- [x] Implement a Markdown linter since that re-uses 99% of the code required for the new spell checker and because I can.
- [x] Write a few code-fold extensions to allow for better code folding, even where we're limited to the older `StreamParser`s
- [x] Better styling for the tooltips (which are now no longer sponsored by tippy but by the library itself)
- [x] Better styling for the autocompletes (strictly speaking also "only" tooltips)
- [x] Re-implement the emphasis-renderer because now we can do that properly (thanks to the AST)
- [x] More careful (secure) inline-HTML rendering (since we can do that now, thanks to the AST)
- [x] Re-implement iFrame rendering
- [x] When the user closes the last tab, empty out the editor
- [x] Add an exemption for the autocorrect to not replace --- with an em dash when it's the first line of the file (collides with YAML frontmatter)
- [x] Spellchecker currently takes sometimes more than a minute to check a word (due to the overhead of approx. 50-70ms per IPC call), I need to bundle multiple words together (basically: string array in, array of booleans out)
- [x] Implement `==.+==`-highlighting
- [x] Fix styles of emphasis/strong emphasis in and outside of lists

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS 12.6 (Silicon/ARM)

## Description
<!-- Below, please describe what the PR does in one or two short sentences. -->
This PR refactors the `getCleanedWords` function to separate text nodes using [`Intl.Segmenter`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Intl/Segmenter). This enables correctly counting words in languages which do not split on whitespace.

This was proposed in #2363, and this PR implements the tests and functionality described in that issue.

Closes #2363

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->
The `getCleanedWords` function was refactored so that text nodes are split using `Intl.Segmenter` rather than on whitespace. 

Character counting was refactored to remove the `map` and `reduce` step, since we can get the character count when generating the word list. The `getCleanedWords` function was refactored so that it returns the word list and the character count, instead of just the word list.

An optional `locale` argument was added to the various word/char counting functions so that the locale can be explicitly set, otherwise, it falls back to get the `appLang` option from `window.config`

Tests were updated for correctness, and new tests were added to test Japanese word counting. The tests were also refactored to include more diagnostic information on failure, including the test number and the locale used.

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->
The following test was actually incorrect, even though it was passing. This line is what I believe is all that should be counted, `# Heading\n\nLorem Ipsum dolor, sit amet`, and it is actually 29 characters, not 30. 

The new implementation counts the number of characters correctly (29).

```ts
{
  input: '---\ntitle: "Some title"\nkeywords:\n  - one\n  - two\n  - three\n...\n\n# Heading\n\nLorem Ipsum dolor, sit amet',
  locale: 'en',
  expectedWords: 6,
  expectedChars: 30
},
  ```
<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: <!-- OS including version -->

closed_issues.summary: [Enhancement] Word counting with UAX #29 word boundaries

description.text:
### Description

Writing notes in Japanese with Zettlr works fine but word counting is far from a satisfactory behavior. In Japanese, you will not insert white spaces between words. Thus, Zettlr counts the whole paragraph as *a word.* Precise Japanese word counting is hard, but the [Unicode Standard Annex #29 defines a language-agnostic algorithm to determine word boundaries.](https://unicode.org/reports/tr29/#Word_Boundaries) This algorithm will not harm users that is already fine with the current behavior, and help users frustrated by the current behavior more or less.

### Proposed Changes

Implementing a word counting method based on the UAX 29's default word boundaries algorithm. Making that algorithm default or not depends on the runtime performance of it.

### Caveats

The UAX 29's default word boundaries algorithm is more complex than white space-based one. On the other hand, the algorithm is not optimal and [CLDR](http://cldr.unicode.org/)-based language-specific word boundaries algorithm will produce better results.

It is excellent to me if the CLDR-based method is implemented, but language-agnostic one is better than the status quo.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Stable (most recent version)

### Your Platform

- [X] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Operating System Version

Windows 11 (Beta)

### Additional Information

_No response_

ranked_scenarios.summary: refactor: Move FSAL parser to utilize the Markdown AST Parser

description.text:
## Description
<!-- Below, please describe what the PR does in one or two short sentences. -->

This PR replaces the FSAL Markdown file parsing logic with our new Markdown AST. This reduces complexity and makes sure that what the file descriptors contain matches what the main editor sees.

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->

* Removed the manual parsing logic from the FSAL file parser
* Utilize the Markdown AST parser to extract the types of nodes that are important for collecting metadata on the files

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->

**Breaking Changes**

- This PR now contains a breaking change: The character count without spaces is now gone. After refactoring, I took the liberty to remove that. The reasons are as follows:
    - I originally implemented that counter because – from a programmer's perspective – a plain character count is meaningful (which is why I included it), but when writing text, what should count is only the amount of non-whitespace characters (which is also the writing target for many teachers and journals)
    - However, since Markdown contains a large amount of non-significant whitespace, or, whitespace that has no bearing on the factual text content of a file (e.g. ending a line with two spaces to signify a single line break), a plain character counter has no real use while writing (academic) texts
    - Thus, **the character counter without spaces is now gone, and the regular character counter now excludes whitespace**
    - This behavior is now unified across the app, so there will only be a single character count, no matter where

There are a few TODOs left:

- [x] I realized that now with the new AST we are able to greatly reduce the complexity of the word counter just as well. So I'll have to go back and do that as well, since it's a no-brainer right now
- [x] Go over the entire code base again and eliminate any remnant of manual parsing of Markdown code
- [x] Remove the old legacy `md2ast` function; also check if there are any other MD parsers left in the code base

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS Ventura 13.3.0

## Description

This PR refactors the main editor in such a way that, instead of one `MarkdownEditor` instance maintaining the entire barrage of documents in the `openFiles` array, the class now only handles a single file.

This has the following benefits:

1. The state management of the `MarkdownEditor` class is simpler: It does not have to cache the various documents, nor does it have to cache the scroll positions of the documents
2. The `MainEditor` Vue component is simpler, since it also doesn't have to implement too much of a switcheroo-logic to ensure the editor contains the correct document. This should make the type of bugs which @efficacy has described, impossible.
3. The entire state logic has now moved to `EditorPane`, i.e. it has been outsourced to the Vue library. This has two additional benefits:
    1. The documents will retain their scroll position, as they are merely hidden instead of completely dropped out of existence – all without any weird caching magic
    2. We now have the ability to add additional editors for various other purposes. Two specific things I would definitely like to add would be a PDF reader and an Image viewer. Further editors/viewers would be possible, but we should remember that Zettlr is not VSCode ;)

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->

* The document provider now emits the previously unused `FILE_SAVED` event, which the `MainEditor` now listens to
* Added documentation for previously undocumented variables of the `MarkdownEditor` class
* Removed the state and view cache from the `MarkdownEditor` class
* Remove any code required to swap documents
* Removed the weird extra-code for the `darkMode` setting which was a remnant from when I initially implemented the editor, since that is handled with the configuration itself by now
* The `MarkdownEditor` instances now expose a property `documentPath` that can be used to query what document they represent
* Exchange the single `MainEditor` component in the `EditorPane` component with a `template v-for`, utilizing `v-show` to show/hide the various editors programmatically, based on which one contains the `activeFile`
* Removed any code from the `MainEditor` component used to differentiate whether it should react to given commands (text insertion, moving around of sections, etc.), and instead this is done in the `EditorPane` component, which will simply pass an unreactive dummy to those instances that should not handle anything
* Refactored some code here and there to make it more readable

## Additional information

This should not include any behavioral changes, except that the state of the various documents is better retained even when they are not active and in the background. The biggest change users should spot are (hopefully) that the scroll positions are now precise.

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS Ventura (13.2.1); MBP 14in M2 Pro
